title: Nature reserve

infobox.value: (also known as a **wildlife refuge**, **wildlife** **sanctuary**, **biosphere reserve** or **bioreserve**, **natural** or **nature preserve**, or **nature [conservation area](/wiki/Conservation_area "Conservation area")**) is a [protected area](/wiki/Protected_area "Protected area") of importance for [flora](/wiki/Flora "Flora"), [fauna](/wiki/Fauna "Fauna"), [funga](/wiki/Funga "Funga"), or features of [geological](/wiki/Geological "Geological") or other special interest, which is reserved and managed for purposes of [conservation](/wiki/Conservation_(ethic) "Conservation (ethic)") and to provide special opportunities for study or [research](/wiki/Research "Research"). They may be designated by [government](/wiki/Government "Government") institutions in some countries, or by private landowners, such as charities and research [institutions](/wiki/Institution "Institution"). Nature reserves fall into different [IUCN categories](/wiki/IUCN_protected_area_categories "IUCN protected area categories") depending on the level of protection afforded by local laws. Normally it is more strictly protected than a [nature park](/wiki/Nature_park "Nature park"). Various jurisdictions may use other terminology, such as **ecological protection area** or [private protected area](/wiki/Private_protected_area "Private protected area") in legislation and in official titles of the reserves.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Protected area for flora, fauna or features of geological interest

"Biological reserve" redirects here. For use of the term specific to Brazil, see [Biological reserve (Brazil)](/wiki/Biological_reserve_(Brazil) "Biological reserve (Brazil)").

"Biosphere reserve" redirects here. For use of the term specific to UNESCO, see [Man and the Biosphere Programme](/wiki/Man_and_the_Biosphere_Programme "Man and the Biosphere Programme").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Jefferson_Memorial_Forest-Bee_Lick_Creek.jpg/250px-Jefferson_Memorial_Forest-Bee_Lick_Creek.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jefferson_Memorial_Forest-Bee_Lick_Creek.jpg)

Bee Lick Creek in the [Jefferson Memorial Forest](/wiki/Jefferson_Memorial_Forest "Jefferson Memorial Forest"), a [National Audubon Society](/wiki/National_Audubon_Society "National Audubon Society") wildlife refuge

A **nature reserve** (also known as a **wildlife refuge**, **wildlife** **sanctuary**, **biosphere reserve** or **bioreserve**, **natural** or **nature preserve**, or **nature [conservation area](/wiki/Conservation_area "Conservation area")**) is a [protected area](/wiki/Protected_area "Protected area") of importance for [flora](/wiki/Flora "Flora"), [fauna](/wiki/Fauna "Fauna"), [funga](/wiki/Funga "Funga"), or features of [geological](/wiki/Geological "Geological") or other special interest, which is reserved and managed for purposes of [conservation](/wiki/Conservation_(ethic) "Conservation (ethic)") and to provide special opportunities for study or [research](/wiki/Research "Research"). They may be designated by [government](/wiki/Government "Government") institutions in some countries, or by private landowners, such as charities and research [institutions](/wiki/Institution "Institution"). Nature reserves fall into different [IUCN categories](/wiki/IUCN_protected_area_categories "IUCN protected area categories") depending on the level of protection afforded by local laws. Normally it is more strictly protected than a [nature park](/wiki/Nature_park "Nature park"). Various jurisdictions may use other terminology, such as **ecological protection area** or [private protected area](/wiki/Private_protected_area "Private protected area") in legislation and in official titles of the reserves.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Charles_Waterton.jpg/250px-Charles_Waterton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Waterton.jpg)

[Charles Waterton](/wiki/Charles_Waterton "Charles Waterton") established the first nature reserve in 1821.

Cultural practices that roughly equate to the establishment and maintenance of reserved areas for animals date back to antiquity, with King [Devanampiya Tissa](/wiki/Devanampiya_Tissa "Devanampiya Tissa") of [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") establishing [Mihintale](/wiki/Mihintale "Mihintale") wildlife sanctuary, one of the world's earliest [wildlife sanctuaries](/wiki/Wildlife_sanctuary "Wildlife sanctuary"), in the 3rd-century-BC Anuradhapura Ancient Kingdom.[[1]](#cite_note-Surveyor_Department-1) Early reservations often had a religious underpinning, such as the 'evil forest' areas of [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") which were forbidden to humans, who were threatened with spiritual attack if they went there. Sacred areas taboo from human entry to fishing and hunting are known by many ancient cultures worldwide.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The world's first modern nature reserve was established in 1821 by the [naturalist](/wiki/Naturalist "Naturalist") and [explorer](/wiki/Explorer "Explorer") [Charles Waterton](/wiki/Charles_Waterton "Charles Waterton") around his estate in [Walton Hall, West Yorkshire](/wiki/Walton_Hall,_West_Yorkshire "Walton Hall, West Yorkshire"). He spent £9000 on the construction of a three-mile long, 9 ft tall wall to enclose his park against [poachers](/wiki/Poaching "Poaching").[[3]](#cite_note-3) He tried to encourage bird life by planting trees and hollowing out trunks for [owls](/wiki/Owl "Owl") to nest in.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Drachenfels_%28Siebengebirge%29_from_Rh%C3%B6ndorf.jpg/250px-Drachenfels_%28Siebengebirge%29_from_Rh%C3%B6ndorf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drachenfels_(Siebengebirge)_from_Rh%C3%B6ndorf.jpg)

[Drachenfels (Siebengebirge)](/wiki/Drachenfels_(Siebengebirge) "Drachenfels (Siebengebirge)")

Waterton invented artificial [nest boxes](/wiki/Nest_box "Nest box") to house [starlings](/wiki/Common_starling "Common starling"), [western jackdaws](/wiki/Western_jackdaw "Western jackdaw") and [sand martins](/wiki/Sand_martin "Sand martin"); and unsuccessfully attempted to introduce [little owls](/wiki/Little_owl "Little owl") from Italy.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Waterton allowed local people access to his reserve and was described by [David Attenborough](/wiki/David_Attenborough "David Attenborough") as "one of the first people anywhere to recognise not only that the natural world was of great importance but that it needed protection as humanity made more and more demands on it".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Drachenfels](/wiki/Drachenfels_(Siebengebirge) "Drachenfels (Siebengebirge)") was protected as the first state-designated nature reserve in modern-day Germany; the site was bought by the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") State in 1836 to protect it from further quarrying.

The first major nature reserve was [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") in [Wyoming, United States](/wiki/Wyoming,_United_States "Wyoming, United States"), followed by the [Royal National Park](/wiki/Royal_National_Park "Royal National Park") near [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), Australia, and the [Barguzin Nature Reserve](/wiki/Barguzin_Nature_Reserve "Barguzin Nature Reserve") of [Imperial Russia](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia"), the first of *[zapovedniks](/wiki/Zapovednik "Zapovednik")* set up by a federal government entirely for the scientific study of nature.[[6]](#cite_note-weiner-6)

## Around the world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Around the world")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/H%C3%A4me_Tower_on_Aulanko_Hill_04.jpg/250px-H%C3%A4me_Tower_on_Aulanko_Hill_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:H%C3%A4me_Tower_on_Aulanko_Hill_04.jpg)

A forest of the Aulanko Reserve[[7]](#cite_note-7) in [Hämeenlinna](/wiki/H%C3%A4meenlinna "Hämeenlinna"), [Kanta-Häme](/wiki/Kanta-H%C3%A4me "Kanta-Häme"), Finland

There are several national and international organizations that oversee the numerous non-profit [animal sanctuaries](/wiki/Animal_sanctuary "Animal sanctuary") and refuges to provide a general system for sanctuaries to follow. Among them, the [American Sanctuary Association](/wiki/American_Sanctuary_Association "American Sanctuary Association") monitors and aids in various facilities to care for exotic wildlife.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The number of sanctuaries has substantially increased over the past few years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Australia")]

See also: [Nature reserve (Australia)](/wiki/Nature_reserve_(Australia) "Nature reserve (Australia)")

In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), a nature reserve is the title of a type of [protected area](/wiki/Protected_area "Protected area") used in the jurisdictions of the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") and [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia"). The term "nature reserve" is defined in the relevant [statutes](/wiki/Statute "Statute") used in those states and territories rather than by a single national statute. As of 2016, 1767 out of a total of 11044 protected areas listed within the Australian [National Reserve System](/wiki/National_Reserve_System "National Reserve System") used the term "nature reserve" in their names.[[9]](#cite_note-CAPAD2014-National-9)

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Brazil")]

In Brazil, nature reserves are classified by the [National System of Nature Conservation Units](/wiki/National_System_of_Nature_Conservation_Units "National System of Nature Conservation Units") as [ecological stations](/wiki/Ecological_station_(Brazil) "Ecological station (Brazil)") ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *estações ecológicas*) or [biological reserves](/wiki/Biological_reserve_(Brazil) "Biological reserve (Brazil)") ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *reservas biológicas*). Their main objectives are preserving fauna and flora and other natural attributes, excluding direct human interference. Visits are allowed only with permission, and only for educational or scientific purposes. Changes to the ecosystems in both types of reserve are allowed to restore and preserve the natural balance, biological diversity and natural ecological processes.[[10]](#cite_note-ICMBio-10) Ecological stations are also allowed to change the environment within strictly defined limits (e.g. affecting no more than three percent of the area or 1,500 hectares (3,700 acres), whichever is less) for the purpose of scientific research.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
A [wildlife reserve in Brazil](/wiki/Wildlife_reserve_(Brazil) "Wildlife reserve (Brazil)") is also protected, and hunting is not allowed, but products and by-products from research may be sold.[[10]](#cite_note-ICMBio-10)

Wildlife refuges in Brazil have as their objective the protection of natural flora and fauna where conditions are assured for the existence and reproduction of species or communities of the local flora and the resident or migratory fauna. The refuges can consist of privately owned land, as long as the objectives of the unit are compatible with the landowners' usage of the land and natural resources. Public visits are subject to the conditions and restrictions established by the management plan of the unit and are subject to authorisation by and regulations of the main administrative and scientific research body.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Canada")]

The Niagara Escarpment and the St. Lawrence River in Ontario are among the 18 nature reserves recognized by UNESCO in Canada.

As a federal government, Canada recognizes 55 National Wildlife Areas, containing species of ecological significance. This area is protected by legislation known as the Canada Wildlife Act, which is overseen by the Ministry of Environment and Climate Change. About half of the habitat is marine habitat, and the remainder is terrestrial habitat.

Nature Conservancy Canada, Ducks Unlimited, and Escarpment Biosphere Conservancy, among others, protect nature reserves in Canada as well. As part of Canada's Ecological Gifts Program, these charities protect wilderness on privately owned lands.

### Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Egypt")]

There are 30 nature reserves in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") which cover 12% of Egyptian land. Those nature reserves were built according to the laws no. 102/1983 and 4/1994 for protection of the Egyptian nature reserve. Egypt announced a plan from to build 40 nature reserves from 1997 to 2017, to help protect the natural resources and the culture and history of those areas. The largest nature reserve in Egypt is [Gebel Elba](/wiki/Gebel_Elba "Gebel Elba") (35,600 square kilometres (13,700 sq mi)) in the southeast, on the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") coast.

### Eritrea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Eritrea")]

On 16 March 1959, the British established the [Yob Wildlife Reserve](/wiki/Yob_Wildlife_Reserve "Yob Wildlife Reserve") in northern Eritrea specifically to protect significant populations of [Nubian ibex](/wiki/Nubian_ibex "Nubian ibex") in the area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Europe

#### Denmark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Denmark")]

Denmark has [three national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Denmark "List of national parks of Denmark") and several nature reserves, some of them inside the national park areas. The largest single reserve is Hanstholm Nature Reserve, which covers 40 km2 (9,900 acres) and is part of [Thy National Park](/wiki/Thy_National_Park "Thy National Park").

#### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sweden")]

In [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), there are [30 national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Sweden "List of national parks of Sweden"). The first of them was established in 1909.[[13]](#cite_note-13) There are almost 4,000 nature reserves in Sweden. They comprise about 85% of the surface that is protected by the Swedish Environmental Code.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

#### Estonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Estonia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Tarvasj%C3%B5gi.jpg/250px-Tarvasj%C3%B5gi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tarvasj%C3%B5gi.jpg)

The [Tarvasjõgi river](/wiki/Tarvasj%C3%B5gi "Tarvasjõgi") at [Põhja-Kõrvemaa Nature Reserve](/wiki/P%C3%B5hja-K%C3%B5rvemaa_Nature_Reserve "Põhja-Kõrvemaa Nature Reserve") in [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia")

In Estonia, there are [five national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Estonia "List of national parks of Estonia"), more than 100 nature reserves, and around 130 landscape protection areas. The largest nature reserve in Estonia is [Alam-Pedja Nature Reserve](/wiki/Alam-Pedja_Nature_Reserve "Alam-Pedja Nature Reserve"), which covers 342 km2 (85,000 acres).

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: France")]

As of 2017, [France](/wiki/France "France") counts [10 national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_France "List of national parks of France"), around 50 [regional nature parks](/wiki/Regional_nature_parks_of_France "Regional nature parks of France"), and 8 [marine parks](/w/index.php?title=Marine_parks&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marine parks (page does not exist)").

#### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Germany")]

In 1995 [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") had 5,314 nature reserves ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *[Naturschutzgebiete](/wiki/Naturschutzgebiete "Naturschutzgebiete")*) covering 6,845 km2 (2,643 sq mi), the largest total areas being in Bavaria with 1,416 km2 (547 sq mi) and Lower Saxony with 1,275 km2 (492 sq mi).

#### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Hungary")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Termeszetvedelmi_terulet.JPG/250px-Termeszetvedelmi_terulet.JPG)](/wiki/File:Termeszetvedelmi_terulet.JPG)

Nature reserve near [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), next to [Lake Naplás](/wiki/Lake_Napl%C3%A1s "Lake Naplás")

In Hungary, there are [10 national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Hungary "List of national parks of Hungary"), more than 15 nature reserves and more than 250 protected areas.
[Hortobágy National Park](/wiki/Hortob%C3%A1gy_National_Park "Hortobágy National Park") is the largest continuous natural grassland in Europe and the oldest national park in Hungary. It is situated on the eastern part of Hungary, on the plain of the Alföld. It was established in 1972. There are alkaline grasslands interrupted by marshes. They have a sizable importance because there are the fishponds. One of the most spectacular sights of the park is the autumn migration of cranes. Some famous Hungarian animal species live in Hortobágy National Park, such as the grey cattle, racka long-wool sheep living only in Hungary, Hungarian horses and buffalo. Hortobágy National Park has been a UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") since 1 December 1999.

#### Kosovo

Main article: [Protected areas of Kosovo](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Kosovo "Protected areas of Kosovo")

In Kosovo, there are 2 [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park"), 11 nature reserves, 99 [natural monuments](/wiki/Natural_monument "Natural monument") and 3 [protected landscapes](/wiki/Protected_landscape "Protected landscape").[[15]](#cite_note-15) The national parks are [Bjeshkët e Nemuna National Park](/wiki/Bjeshk%C3%ABt_e_Nemuna_National_Park "Bjeshkët e Nemuna National Park") and [Sharr Mountains National Park](/wiki/Sharr_Mountains_National_Park "Sharr Mountains National Park").

#### Portugal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Portugal")]

Main article: [Protected areas of Portugal](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Portugal "Protected areas of Portugal")

Nature reserves are one of the 11 types of protected areas in Portugal. As of 2012, Portugal had a total of 46 protected areas, which represented 6,807.89 km2 (2,628.54 sq mi) of land and 463.94 km2 (179.13 sq mi) of marine surfaces. Among the protected areas, nine are classified as nature reserve ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *reserva natural*).[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### Romania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Romania")]

Main article: [Protected areas of Romania](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Romania "Protected areas of Romania")

About 5.18% of the area of Romania has a protected status (12,360 km2 (4,770 sq mi)), including the [Danube Delta](/wiki/Danube_Delta "Danube Delta"), which makes up half of this area (2.43% of Romania's total area).

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Spain")]

See also: [List of national parks of Spain](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Spain "List of national parks of Spain")

There are 15 National Parks, and around 90 Natural Parks in Spain. Spain is the country with the most sites listed in the [World Network of Biosphere Reserves](/wiki/World_Network_of_Biosphere_Reserves "World Network of Biosphere Reserves").

#### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

There are 4 biosphere reserves (two of them are dated 1927 and 1874) and 17 nature reserves in Ukraine, covering 160,000 ha (400,000 acres). Ukraine administers [40 national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Ukraine "List of national parks of Ukraine"), 2632 habitat management areas, 3025 nature monuments, and 1430 other preservations.

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

There are some differences between the regulations for [England](/wiki/England "England"), [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), which are separately managed.

At the end of March 2004, there were 215 [national nature reserves in England](/wiki/National_nature_reserves_in_England "National nature reserves in England") with a total area of 879 km2 (339 sq mi). The reserves are scattered through England, from [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") in [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland") to [The Lizard](/wiki/The_Lizard "The Lizard") in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"). Nearly every rural county has at least one. Many national nature reserves contain nationally important populations of rare [flowers](/wiki/Flower "Flower"), [ferns](/wiki/Fern "Fern") and [mosses](/wiki/Moss "Moss"), [butterflies](/wiki/Butterflies "Butterflies") and other [insects](/wiki/Insect "Insect"), and nesting and wintering [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird"). Examples include unique [alpine plants](/wiki/Alpine_plant "Alpine plant") at [Upper Teesdale](/wiki/Upper_Teesdale "Upper Teesdale") and the field of [snake's head fritillaries](/wiki/Fritillaria_meleagris "Fritillaria meleagris") at [North Meadow, Cricklade](/wiki/North_Meadow,_Cricklade "North Meadow, Cricklade"), [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire "Wiltshire").

There are now over 1,050 [local nature reserves](/wiki/Local_nature_reserve "Local nature reserve") in England. They range from windswept coastal headlands, ancient woodlands and flower-rich meadows to former inner-city railways, long-abandoned landfill sites and industrial areas now re-colonized by wildlife. In total, they cover almost 40,000 ha (99,000 acres)—a natural resource which makes an important contribution to England's [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity"). A good example is [Rye Harbour Nature Reserve](/wiki/Rye_Harbour_SSSI "Rye Harbour SSSI") in East Sussex, where a network of footpaths enables visitors to explore shingle, saltmarsh, saline lagoon, reedbed, and grazing marsh habitats.

Through the Natural Heritage (Scotland) Act 1991 the Scottish Natural Heritage (SNH) was established in 1992 as a government body, responsible to the Scottish Government Ministers and through them to the Scottish Parliament. At 31 March 2008, there were 65 [Scottish national nature reserves](/wiki/National_nature_reserve_(Scotland) "National nature reserve (Scotland)") with a total area of approximately 1,330 km2 (510 sq mi). Section 21 of the [National Parks and Access to the Countryside Act 1949](/wiki/National_Parks_and_Access_to_the_Countryside_Act_1949 "National Parks and Access to the Countryside Act 1949") gives local authorities the exclusive statutory power to establish a [local nature reserve](/wiki/List_of_local_nature_reserves_in_Scotland "List of local nature reserves in Scotland") in consultation with the SNH.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Iran

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Iran")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Kohgiluyeh_and_Boyer-Ahmad_Province%2C_Iran_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Kohgiluyeh_and_Boyer-Ahmad_Province%2C_Iran_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kohgiluyeh_and_Boyer-Ahmad_Province,_Iran_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Dena](/wiki/Dena "Dena"), a biosphere reserve in Iran

Main article: [Biosphere reserves of Iran](/wiki/Biosphere_reserves_of_Iran "Biosphere reserves of Iran")

The biosphere reserves of Iran have a total land area of 1.64 million km2. The "reserves" support more than 8,000 recorded species of plants (almost 2,421 are [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism")), 502 species of [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird"), 164 species of [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal"), 209 species of [reptiles](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile"), and 375 species of [butterflies](/wiki/Butterfly "Butterfly").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: India")]

Main article: [Biosphere reserves of India](/wiki/Biosphere_reserves_of_India "Biosphere reserves of India")

[India](/wiki/India "India")'s 18 biospheres extend over a total of 85,940 km2 (33,180 sq mi) and protect larger areas than typical national parks in other countries. The first national reserve of India was established in 1986.

### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Israel")]

Main article: [Nature reserves of Israel](/wiki/Nature_reserves_of_Israel "Nature reserves of Israel")

[Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel")'s national parks are declared historic sites or nature reserves, which are mostly operated and maintained by the National Nature and Parks Authority. As of 2019, Israel maintains more than 490 nature reserves that protect 2,500 species of indigenous wild plants, 20 species of fish, 530 species of birds and 70 species of mammals. In total, they cover 6,400 km2 (2,500 sq mi) of nature reserves, approximately 28% of the country's land area. In 1984, the two areas with the highest number of nature reserves were the South (15.2%) and [Samaria](/wiki/Samaria "Samaria") (the Shomron, 13.5%).[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Japan")]

Under the Nature Conservation Law, places can be designated as 'wilderness areas', 'nature conservation areas' and 'prefectural nature conservation areas'. In 1995, when the Japanese Government published its information in English, there were 5 wilderness areas, 10 nature conservation areas and 516 prefectural nature conservation areas.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Jordan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Jordan")]

Main article: [Nature reserves in Jordan](/wiki/Nature_reserves_in_Jordan "Nature reserves in Jordan")

There are seven nature reserves in [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"). In 1966 the organization that would later start Jordan's nature reserves, the [Royal Society for the Conservation of Nature](/wiki/Royal_Society_for_the_Conservation_of_Nature "Royal Society for the Conservation of Nature"), was founded. RSCN's first efforts involved bringing back severely endangered species. In 1973, RSCN was given the right to issue hunting licenses, giving RSCN an upper hand in preventing extinction. The first step was the founding of Jordan's first nature reserve, [Shaumari Wildlife Reserve](/wiki/Shaumari_Wildlife_Reserve "Shaumari Wildlife Reserve"), in 1975. The primary purpose was to create means to breed endangered species, specifically: the [Arabian oryx](/wiki/Arabian_oryx "Arabian oryx"), [gazelles](/wiki/Gazelles "Gazelles"), [ostriches](/wiki/Ostriches "Ostriches"), and [Persian onagers](/wiki/Persian_onager "Persian onager") in their natural environment.

### Kyrgyzstan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Kyrgyzstan")]

By the end of 2009 there were 10 nature reserves ([Kyrgyz](/wiki/Kyrgyz_language "Kyrgyz language"): корук, *koruk*) in Kyrgyzstan covering 600,000 hectares (6,000 km2) or about three percent of the total area of the country.

### Mexico, Belize, Guatamala

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Mexico, Belize, Guatamala")]

In 2025, these three countries developed a tri-national nature reserve, the [Corredor Biocultural Gran Selva Maya](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corredor_Biocultural_Gran_Selva_Maya "es:Corredor Biocultural Gran Selva Maya"), which is the second largest nature reserve in the Americas.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Main article: [Protected areas of New Zealand](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_New_Zealand "Protected areas of New Zealand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Tramping_or_hiking_in_Aoraki_Mount_Cook_National_Park..jpg/250px-Tramping_or_hiking_in_Aoraki_Mount_Cook_National_Park..jpg)](/wiki/File:Tramping_or_hiking_in_Aoraki_Mount_Cook_National_Park..jpg)

[Aoraki / Mount Cook National Park](/wiki/Aoraki_/_Mount_Cook_National_Park "Aoraki / Mount Cook National Park") in New Zealand

New Zealand has a variety of types of reserve, including [national parks](/wiki/National_parks_of_New_Zealand "National parks of New Zealand"), various types of conservation areas (including stewardship land that is yet to be officially classified), and seven specific types of "reserve", each of which prioritize various degrees of [protection](/wiki/Environmental_protection "Environmental protection") to different amenities such as scenery, recreation, flora and fauna, scientific value, or history. Land is often sub-categorised beneath its general classification, as defined in law between the *[Reserves Act of 1977](/wiki/Reserves_Act_1977 "Reserves Act 1977")*, the *[National Parks Act of 1980](/wiki/National_Parks_Act_1980_(New_Zealand) "National Parks Act 1980 (New Zealand)")*, and the *[Conservation Act of 1987](/wiki/Conservation_Act_1987 "Conservation Act 1987")*. Under these classifications, the [Department of Conservation](/wiki/New_Zealand_Department_of_Conservation "New Zealand Department of Conservation") administers more than 80,000 square kilometres (31,000 sq mi)—nearly 30% of the nation's total area—with at least some degree of protection. This land is composed of 14 National Parks, 30 Conservation Parks, and approximately 8,900 discrete areas of land in total.

Although the most public land is strongly protected for natural preservation, the term *nature reserve* is specifically defined in the Reserves Act to mean a reserve that prioritizes the protection of rare flora and fauna, to the extent that public access is by permit only. Some of these reserves include [Ecological Islands](/wiki/Ecological_Island "Ecological Island"), a comparatively new concept in wildlife preservation, pioneered in New Zealand to help rebuild the populations of nearly extinct birds, and other species that are heavily threatened by introduced predators.

### Nicaragua

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Nicaragua")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/PenasBlancas%2C_part_of_the_Bosawas_Reserve%2C_Jinotega_Department%2C_Nicaragua.jpg/250px-PenasBlancas%2C_part_of_the_Bosawas_Reserve%2C_Jinotega_Department%2C_Nicaragua.jpg)](/wiki/File:PenasBlancas,_part_of_the_Bosawas_Reserve,_Jinotega_Department,_Nicaragua.jpg)

Peñas Blancas, part of the [Bosawás Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Bosaw%C3%A1s_Biosphere_Reserve "Bosawás Biosphere Reserve") is the second largest rainforest in the Western Hemisphere, after the Amazonian Rainforest in Brazil. Located northeast of the city of [Jinotega](/wiki/Jinotega "Jinotega") in Northeastern Nicaragua.

In [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), the [Ministry of the Environment and Natural Resources](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Environment_and_Natural_Resources "Ministry of the Environment and Natural Resources") (MARENA) is in charge of environmental protection and of the study, planning, and management of Nicaragua's natural resources. Nearly one-fifth of the territory is designated as protected areas like national parks, nature reserves (including the [Bosawás Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Bosaw%C3%A1s_Biosphere_Reserve "Bosawás Biosphere Reserve")), and biological reserves. Nicaragua has [78 protected areas](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Nicaragua "Protected areas of Nicaragua") that cover 22,422 km2 (8,657 sq mi), about 17.3% of the nation's landmass. Private nature reserves exist with land excluded from private land trusts and maintained at the sole cost of the proprietor. For example, O Parks, WildLife, and Recreation, Or [El Ostional](/wiki/El_Ostional "El Ostional") Private Wildlife Reserve, was established within the [Mesoamerican Biological Corridor](/wiki/Mesoamerican_Biological_Corridor "Mesoamerican Biological Corridor") by retired [FDNY](/wiki/FDNY "FDNY") firefighter Kevin Michael Shea, who purchased 46 acres (0.19 km2) of land for the purpose of restoring the ecological system of a [dry tropical forest](/wiki/Dry_tropical_forest "Dry tropical forest"), ravaged during the [Nicaraguan Revolution](/wiki/Nicaraguan_Revolution "Nicaraguan Revolution"). The [park](/wiki/Park "Park") provides a private nature reserve, [wildlife corridor](/wiki/Wildlife_corridor "Wildlife corridor") and verified [carbon credits](/wiki/Carbon_credits "Carbon credits").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main article: [Zapovednik](/wiki/Zapovednik "Zapovednik")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/%D0%9B%D0%B5%D1%81_%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BA%D1%80%D1%83%D0%B3_%D0%B3.%D0%AF%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%83.jpg/250px-%D0%9B%D0%B5%D1%81_%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BA%D1%80%D1%83%D0%B3_%D0%B3.%D0%AF%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%83.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9B%D0%B5%D1%81_%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BA%D1%80%D1%83%D0%B3_%D0%B3.%D0%AF%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%83.jpg)

[South Ural Nature Reserve](/wiki/South_Ural_Nature_Reserve "South Ural Nature Reserve") in Russia

There are around 100 nature reserves ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): заповедник, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *zapovednik*) in Russia, covering some 330,000 km2 (130,000 sq mi), or about 1.4% of the country's total area. A few of them predate the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") of 1917, but most have been created during the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") era. There are also natural protected areas where only certain species are protected, or only certain activities are prohibited; those are known as *zakaznik* (Russian: заказник).

Unofficial sanctuaries can also occur as a result of human accidents; the [Chernobyl Exclusion Zone](/wiki/Chernobyl_Exclusion_Zone "Chernobyl Exclusion Zone") has in practice become a wildlife refuge since very few people live in the area. Wildlife has flourished in the zone since the Chernobyl nuclear accident in 1986.[[25]](#cite_note-BBCChern-25)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: South Africa")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Hills_and_valleys_%28207920050%29.jpg/250px-Hills_and_valleys_%28207920050%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hills_and_valleys_(207920050).jpg)

Matjulu waterhole near [Berg-en-dal](/wiki/Berg-en-Dal_Camp "Berg-en-Dal Camp") in the Kruger National Park

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Clarens_Drive_-_Cape_Whale_Coast_-_Western_Cape.jpg/250px-Clarens_Drive_-_Cape_Whale_Coast_-_Western_Cape.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clarens_Drive_-_Cape_Whale_Coast_-_Western_Cape.jpg)

With ≈9000 plant species found in ≈78km², the [Cape Floristic Region](/wiki/Cape_Floristic_Region "Cape Floristic Region") has a concentration of plants that is greater than anywhere else on earth.

Main articles: [South African National Parks](/wiki/South_African_National_Parks "South African National Parks") and [List of protected areas of South Africa](/wiki/List_of_protected_areas_of_South_Africa "List of protected areas of South Africa")

South Africa is well known for its many nature reserves. The oldest nature reserve in the country (and oldest conservation area in the world) is the [Groenkloof Nature Reserve](/wiki/Groenkloof_Nature_Reserve "Groenkloof Nature Reserve") that was established in 1892 in the capital city [Pretoria](/wiki/Pretoria "Pretoria") in the old [South African Republic](/wiki/South_African_Republic "South African Republic") and current Republic of South Africa.

The country has many national parks but the best-known is the [Kruger National Park](/wiki/Kruger_National_Park "Kruger National Park"), which was announced in 1898, and is the largest, at nearly 2,000,000 ha (20,000 km2). The Kruger Park and [Table Mountain National Park](/wiki/Table_Mountain_National_Park "Table Mountain National Park") are two of South Africa's most visited tourist attractions, along with the [Addo Elephant National Park](/wiki/Addo_Elephant_National_Park "Addo Elephant National Park").

South Africa also has 10 [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_South_Africa "List of World Heritage Sites in South Africa"), including four natural sites and one mixed site. And it has provincial [game reserves](/wiki/Game_reserve "Game reserve") including [Shamwari](/wiki/Shamwari_Game_Reserve "Shamwari Game Reserve"), [Londolozi](/wiki/Londolozi_Private_Game_Reserve "Londolozi Private Game Reserve"), Sanbona and Lalibela. The country currently has 20 national parks covering 3,700,000 ha (37,000 km2), about 3% of the total area of South Africa.

The [Prince Edward Islands](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Islands "Prince Edward Islands"), which are South African territories in the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean"), have been declared a special nature reserve. It is a highly protected area from which all human activity is excluded, except for conservation and scientific research.

### Sri Lanka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Sri Lanka")]

The area around [Mihintale](/wiki/Mihintale "Mihintale"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), was a sanctuary for wildlife,[[26]](#cite_note-26) probably the first of its generation in the ancient world. According to stone inscriptions found in the vicinity, the king commanded the people not to harm animals or destroy trees within the area.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nature_reserve&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/USFWS_cascade_head_%2823768226491%29.jpg/250px-USFWS_cascade_head_%2823768226491%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:USFWS_cascade_head_(23768226491).jpg)

[Cascade Head](/wiki/Cascade_Head "Cascade Head"), [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), United States, is a [UNESCO biosphere reserve](/wiki/Man_and_the_Biosphere_Programme "Man and the Biosphere Programme").

In the United States, the [U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service](/wiki/United_States_Fish_and_Wildlife_Service "United States Fish and Wildlife Service"), managed by the [Secretary of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior"), is responsible for managing many of the federal nature reserves including the [National Wildlife Refuge System](/wiki/National_Wildlife_Refuge "National Wildlife Refuge"). The National Wildlife Refuge System includes areas administered for the protection and conservation of fish and wildlife that are threatened with extinction, as well as wildlife ranges, game ranges, wildlife management areas, and waterfowl production areas.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The first North American wildlife refuge, Lake Merritt Wildlife sanctuary at [Lake Merritt](/wiki/Lake_Merritt "Lake Merritt"), was established by [Samuel Merritt](/wiki/Samuel_Merritt "Samuel Merritt") and enacted in California state law in 1870 as the first government owned refuge.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The first federally owned refuge in the United States is [Pelican Island National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Pelican_Island_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Pelican Island National Wildlife Refuge") and was established by [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") in 1903 as part of his [Square Deal](/wiki/Square_Deal "Square Deal") campaign to improve the country. At the time, setting aside land for wildlife was not a [constitutional](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") right of the president. In 2006, a bi-partisan group of [US House of Representatives](/wiki/US_House_of_Representatives "US House of Representatives") members established the [Congressional Wildlife Refuge Caucus](/wiki/Congressional_Wildlife_Refuge_Caucus "Congressional Wildlife Refuge Caucus") to further support the needs of the National Wildlife Refuge System in [Congress](/wiki/US_Congress "US Congress").

There are also state-level administered State Nature Reserves found throughout the country, as well as smaller reserves operated by local governments, private trusts, or even funded through public donations. Private nature reserves also exist, with land excluded from private land trusts and maintained at the sole cost of the proprietor, such as the 1,800 acres (730 ha) [Wilbur Hot Springs](/wiki/Wilbur_Hot_Springs "Wilbur Hot Springs") and the Unexpected Nature Reserve set up by [Hope Sawyer Buyukmihci](/wiki/Hope_Sawyer_Buyukmihci "Hope Sawyer Buyukmihci").
